per_interval_and_the_bottom_line_incre: per interval and the bottom line increases by
enter_an_amount_greater_than_zero_for_: Enter an amount greater than zero for both options.
both_options_have_the_same_cost_per_un: Both options have the same cost per unit.
option_a_has_the_lower_cost_per_unit: Option A has the lower cost per unit.
option_b_has_the_lower_cost_per_unit: Option B has the lower cost per unit.
adjust_the_values_so_both_unit_rates_s: Adjust the values so both unit rates stay within the learning-model range.
a_fair_comparison_divides_each_cost_by: A fair comparison divides each cost by its own quantity. Context may also involve quality, waste, or other needs.
this_example_is_above_the_1000_learnin: This example is above the 1000% learning-model limit. Adjust the part or whole.
this_result_is_above_the_1_000_000_qua: This result is above the 1,000,000 quantity limit. Adjust the example values.
the_percent_is_not_defined_when_the_wh: The percent is not defined when the whole is zero. Enter a positive whole to build the tape.
the_whole_is_not_defined_when_the_perc: The whole is not defined when the percent is zero. Enter a positive percent to build the tape.
ten_equal_sections_each_represent_10_o: Ten equal sections each represent 10% of one whole.
learning_model_range_0_to_1000_quantit: Learning-model range: 0% to 1000%; quantities up to 1,000,000.
each_outlined_section_is_exactly_10_of: Each outlined section is exactly 10% of one whole. A partially filled section shows the exact fraction of that 10% section.
enter_at_least_two_valid_coordinate_pa: Enter at least two valid coordinate pairs, including one with a nonzero x-value.
proportional_the_unit_rate_is_constant: Proportional: the unit rate is constant.
not_proportional_the_evidence_does_not: Not proportional: the evidence does not show one constant unit rate through the origin.
use_the_same_number_of_x_and_y_values_: Use the same number of x- and y-values. This first-quadrant graph requires a nonnegative number in every row.
fix_the_coordinate_list_before_making_: Fix the coordinate list before making a proportionality decision:
a_proportional_model_ray_is_extended_t: A proportional model ray is extended through the origin.
no_complete_set_of_points_is_available: No complete set of points is available to graph.
table_evidence_all_defined_y_x_values_: Table evidence: all defined y ÷ x values equal
the_entered_points_include_0_0_and_fol: the entered points include (0,0) and follow the model ray.
the_model_ray_extends_the_constant_rel: the model ray extends the constant relationship through (0,0), even though the origin was not entered.
check_for_changing_y_x_values_a_nonzer: Check for changing y ÷ x values, a nonzero y-value when x = 0, or points that do not lie on one straight line through the origin.
hint_align_known_values_first: First align the known values, then locate a helpful unit or scale step.
hint_rewrite_each_situation_per_one: Rewrite each situation per one unit before comparing.
hint_identify_part_percent_and_whole: Identify the part, percent, and whole before choosing an operation.
hint_compare_y_divided_by_x: Compare y divided by x and check whether the graph follows one line through the origin.
hint_find_a_common_scale_factor: Look for one scale factor that changes both quantities together.
move_between_representations_and_expla: Move between representations and explain the multiplicative relationship.
best_for_listing_equivalent_pairs: Best for listing many equivalent pairs and spotting a common scale factor.
best_for_aligned_quantities: Best for seeing aligned quantities, intermediate values, and how far each scale travels.
best_for_fair_comparisons_because_ever: Best for fair comparisons because every situation is rewritten per one unit.
a_ratio_measured_against_a_whole_of_10: A ratio measured against a whole of 100; use part = percent × whole.
a_straight_line_through_the_origin_sho: A straight line through the origin shows one constant multiplier from x to y.
estimate_first_preserve_units_and_ask_: Estimate first, preserve units, and ask whether both quantities changed multiplicatively.
